_schema.purpose: Registry of official City of Berkeley policy statements — adopted fiscal policies, debt policies, reserve policies, and similar governing documents. Policy statements are a distinct and high-value source class: they represent the council's own stated commitments. When auditors find gaps, or when council behavior contradicts a stated policy, the deviation is attributable at a higher confidence level than deviation from external best practices alone.

status_values.pending_review: Policy identified but not yet obtained or extracted
status_values.extracted: Key provisions captured in source extract file
status_values.gap_documented: A gap between stated policy and council behavior or auditor finding has been formally noted

fields.document_ref: Unique key; matches extract filename in sources/policies/
fields.title: Official policy title
fields.adopted_date: Date the council formally adopted this policy (often unknown — policies are not reliably datestamped)
fields.last_amended: Date of most recent amendment, if known
fields.downloaded_date: Date the source document was obtained; used as subfolder name (sources/policies/YYYY-MM-DD/)
fields.key_provisions: What the policy commits the council to
fields.documented_gaps: Gaps identified by the City Auditor or observable from council behavior
fields.scoring_relevance: How this policy connects to taxpayer alignment scoring
fields.source_extract: Path to detailed extract file, if created

fiscal_policy.title: City of Berkeley Fiscal Policies
fiscal_policy.source_url_note: Not a standalone PDF on berkeleyca.gov. Fiscal policies are disclosed in budget transmittal letters and ACFR transmittal letters — extracted versions are in sources/acfr/acfr_fy2023.json (fiscal_policies_fy2023), sources/acfr/acfr_fy2025.json (fiscal_policies_stated).
fiscal_policy.scope: Governing framework for budget preparation, reserve management, debt issuance, and long-term financial planning
documented_gaps: Does not require assessing whether recurring revenues are sufficient to cover recurring expenditures — identified as a gap by the City Auditor in the April 2026 financial condition report (p.22). This is a foundational GFOA best practice.
Policy #5 (one-time revenue for one-time expenditures) violated by Section 115 Trust drawdowns for recurring operating pension expenses in FY2025 and FY2026 — documented in acfr_fy2025.json.
fiscal_policy.scoring_relevance: The fiscal policy is the council's own rulebook. A gap in the rulebook is the council's responsibility to close — particularly the GFOA recurring balance requirement, which has been flagged by the auditor. No member has moved to amend the policy.

debt_management_policy.title: Debt Management and Disclosure Policy
debt_management_policy.last_amended_note: Revised via consent calendar June 27, 2023. Sole substantive change: GO bond debt limit reduced from 15% to 6% of assessed value. No floor discussion.
debt_management_policy.scope: Governs when and how the city issues debt — GO bonds, lease revenue notes, TEFRA bonds, etc. Also governs continuing disclosure obligations to bondholders.
key_provisions: Long-term debt shall NOT be used for operating costs — only capital assets.
GO bond debt limit: 6% of assessed value (~$1.48B capacity at FY2023 assessed value; $205M currently outstanding).
Official Statements require council approval as new business — not consent.
Policy review at least every 5 years.
documented_gaps: The debt capacity framework revision was adopted on the consent calendar with no council discussion — a structural financial policy passed without debate.

Official Statements (bond disclosure docs) are designated new business, but the policy governing all debt issuance was passed on consent — the policy treats disclosure as more significant than the framework itself.
Taplin's November 2025 'Advanced Fiscal Policies — Bond Schedule' referral could result in a regular borrowing cadence embedded in policy — potentially also passed on consent.
debt_management_policy.scoring_relevance: The consent calendar adoption of debt capacity policy is a governance pattern. The explicit prohibition on using long-term debt for operating costs is the key guardrail as structural deficits grow.

reserve_policy.title: General Fund Reserve Policy
reserve_policy.last_amended_note: Resolution No. 71-908-N.S. lowered target from 30% to 20-30% of GF revenues — converting non-compliance into compliance without adding reserves.
reserve_policy.source_url_note: URL is dated 2022-01 — predates the July 2025 amendment. Fetched version reflects pre-amendment policy; amendment is documented in acfr_fy2025.json.
reserve_policy.scope: Governs target reserve levels (Stability + Catastrophic), permissible uses, withdrawal process, and replenishment requirements.
key_provisions: Withdrawal requires a 2/3 vote of City Council.
Stability Reserve (55%): for revenue shortfalls only — NOT for new programs.
Catastrophic Reserve (45%): for emergencies only — explicitly NOT for operational shortfalls.
Original long-term target: 30% of GF revenues by 2027 (within 10 years of 2017 adoption).
Replenishment: schedule required at time of withdrawal; repayment begins within 5 years, complete within 10 years.
Reserve is NOT for ongoing or new programs or services.
documented_gaps: 2017 target was 30% of GF revenues by 2027. July 2025 amendment lowered to 20-30% — council is ~$27M short of original target but now compliant with amended target.

Workers' comp and Section 115 transfers cited by City Auditor as one-time measures — these appear to come from internal service fund balances, not the named Reserves, bypassing the 2/3 withdrawal requirement.
Catastrophic Reserve prohibition on 'operational shortfalls' is categorical — any use of named reserves for recurring expenses would be a policy violation.
reserve_policy.scoring_relevance: The July 2025 amendment is itself a scored event — moving the target after failing to reach it. The 2/3 withdrawal requirement creates a meaningful governance protection, but the one-time balancing measures may be structured to avoid triggering it.

investment_policy.title: City of Berkeley Investment Policy
investment_policy.last_amended_note: Submitted to Council annually. FY2026 version extracted.
investment_policy.scope: All non-trust funds — General Fund and other pooled funds. City and Rent Stabilization Board.
key_provisions: Priority order: Safety > Liquidity > Yield.
Benchmark: 'yields on City investments will regularly meet or exceed the average return on LAIF.'
LAIF cap: $40M.
Extensive responsible investing restrictions: fossil fuels, weapons, guns, prisons, border wall, ESG — none of which apply to LAIF itself.
Quarterly reports to Council within 60 days; must include LAIF comparison.
New investment types require 2 weeks prior Council notification.
documented_gaps: 9 consecutive quarters below LAIF as of Q1 FY2026 — the policy states returns should 'regularly meet or exceed' LAIF. This is sustained non-compliance with the policy's stated yield objective.

Responsible investing constraints reduce the investable universe below what LAIF can access — the benchmark is achievable by LAIF but not necessarily by a portfolio subject to these restrictions. The cost of this policy choice has never been publicly quantified.
LAIF cap of $40M means the city cannot simply 'go to LAIF' with a $200M+ portfolio — the benchmark is structurally unachievable in full.
investment_policy.scoring_relevance: The policy explicitly requires LAIF-matching returns. 9 quarters of non-compliance with quarterly reports to Council means the oversight mechanism exists but has not been exercised. The responsible investing restrictions are a council choice with a quantifiable cost.

retiree_trust_investment_policy.title: City of Berkeley Investment Policy for Retiree Medical Plan Trust Funds
retiree_trust_investment_policy.scope: Retiree Medical Plan Trust assets only. Separate from pooled portfolio.
key_provisions: Prudent risk-taking is explicitly endorsed — different from pooled portfolio's safety-first posture.
Actuarial target return: 7% (policy acknowledges returns are 'significantly below' this target).
Body text authorizes up to 100% in equity mutual funds, index funds, preferred stock, and bond funds.
Appendix A (operative table) limits equity to 25% — creating an internal contradiction within the policy.
Maximum maturity: 30 years (vs. 10 years for pooled portfolio).
No responsible investing restrictions — broader investable universe than pooled portfolio.
No LAIF benchmark — actuarial 7% target is the operative standard.
documented_gaps: Internal contradiction: body text says 100% equity permitted; Appendix A says 25%. Both versions of the policy (pre- and post-July 2025) contain this contradiction. Council has approved this annually without resolving it.
Actuarial target of 7% is not being met — recent returns of 4.64-5.08% mean the trust is underfunded relative to benefit obligations.
No responsible investing restrictions — council applies different values-based constraints to the two portfolios without public explanation of the asymmetry.
retiree_trust_investment_policy.scoring_relevance: The retiree trust's higher returns vs. the pooled portfolio (4.64-5.08% vs. 2.90-3.31%) show better returns are achievable under city authority. The gap is a policy design choice. The internal 100%/25% contradiction is a governance failure that has persisted through multiple annual approvals.

section_115_trust_policy.title: Section 115 Pension Prefunding Trust Policy
section_115_trust_policy.source_url_note: Not found as a standalone PDF at berkeleyca.gov/your-government/financial-information/financial-reports-and-policies. Policy terms are partially documented in ACFR transmittal letters — see acfr_fy2023.json (fiscal policy #11: 'Allocating annual CalPERS prepayment savings to Section 115 Trust up to $5.5M per fiscal year') and acfr_fy2025.json.
section_115_trust_policy.scope: Governs contributions to and withdrawals from the Section 115 pension pre-funding trust
documented_gaps: The trust met its annual contribution goal in only 2 of 7 years per the April 2026 audit. The $3M FY2025 and $6M FY2026 withdrawals to balance operations may or may not have been within policy parameters.
If the policy permits operational withdrawals, it is not functioning as a true pension stabilization reserve.
section_115_trust_policy.scoring_relevance: A trust that is supposed to pre-fund pension obligations being used to balance operating budgets is a category error. Whether the council authorized this knowingly, or whether it happened under policy language that permitted it without explicit council action, is the key question.
